title: Chapter 5: Spore
content: Liang Feng cornered him in the garden at midnight.

Ren had gone for a walk—not for exercise, but because the Salvation System had detected an array fluctuation in the estate's eastern boundary and he needed to triangulate its source without alerting the household security. The gardens were sprawling, manicured, and strategically dark: perfect for a man who needed to move unseen.
He didn't hear Feng approaching. That was the first warning sign.

In the three weeks since the banquet, Ren had mapped every person's movement pattern in this household. The servants rotated on precise schedules. The guards patrolled in overlapping circuits. Feng, specifically, spent his evenings in the entertainment room, drinking imported whisky and playing video games until two a.m.
Feng did not walk the gardens at midnight. Feng did not move silently. And Feng did not materialize from the shadows like something that had learned to hunt.
But here he was, blocking the garden path, moonlight catching the blade in his hand.
"You've been asking questions." Feng's voice was wrong—not the petulant whine Ren had come to expect, but something flatter. Emptier. "About me. About my health."
The knife was a family heirloom— Ren recognized the type. Folded steel, ivory handle, passed down through three generations of Liang men. It could open a man from belly to breastbone.
"I asked if you were sleeping," Ren said calmly. "That's not asking about your health."
"Don't play word games with me." Feng stepped closer. The moonlight caught his eyes, and Ren's stomach dropped.
Feng's left eye was flecked with gold.

Not amber, not haziness—*gold*, metallic and wrong, the way a television pixelates when the signal corrupts. It was subtle enough that you'd miss it in daylight. At midnight, with the moon behind him, it looked like a dying star.
The Salvation System screamed:

`🚨 CRITICAL ALERT: ABYSS SPORE DETECTION`
`SUBJECT: Liang Feng`
`INFECTION STAGE: 2 (Confirmed)`
`PROGRESSION RATE: Accelerated (estimated 9 months to Stage 3)`
`SYMPTOMS: Heightened aggression, parasitic personality overlay, intermittent loss of conscious control`
`THREAT: HOSTILE`
Nine months. Not thirteen. The infection was ahead of schedule.
"Feng." Ren kept his voice level. "Put the knife down."

"Why?" The word came out strange—wet, almost gurgling, as if Feng's throat was producing sounds his brain hadn't authorized. "You think you can tell me what to do? You think you're *special* because my sister hasn't divorced you yet?"

Ren calculated. At 0.01% of his War God power, he couldn't overpower a healthy twenty-two-year-old in a straight fight. But Feng wasn't healthy. The spore infection chipped away at fine motor control and spatial awareness even as it enhanced strength and aggression. That meant Feng would be faster but less precise. Predictable in intensity, unpredictable in direction.
*Let him swing first. Dodge. Disarm. Don't kill him.*
The War God in him added, reluctantly: *And don't break anything that can't heal. He's not the enemy. Not yet.*
"I don't think I'm special," Ren said. "I think you're in trouble, and I'm the only person in this house who's noticed."
Feng's hand trembled. The knife wavered between threatening and something else—something desperate.
"What do you mean?"

"I mean you haven't been sleeping. You've been having dreams you can't explain. There's a pressure in your chest that gets worse when you're angry. Sometimes you black out for a few seconds and come back not knowing what you did." Ren held his gaze. "Am I wrong?"
The garden went very still.
Feng's face contorted—not with rage, but with the expression of a man whose deepest, most secret terror has just been laid bare by a stranger. His lips moved, but no sound came out.
Then something *shifted* behind his eyes.

The gold flecks flared. His pupils dilated to inhuman width. His posture changed—shoulders squaring, chin lifting, weight redistributing with the mechanical precision of something learning to pilot a new body.
"I know you."
The voice wasn't Feng's. It was older, colder, and it resonated at a frequency that made Ren's teeth ache.
The Salvation System:

`⚠️ ENTITY MANIFESTATION DETECTED`
`CLASS: Abyss Parasitic Consciousness (Minor)`
`CONTROL LEVEL: Partial overlay — host consciousness suppressed but not eliminated`
`DANGER: Do NOT engage directly. Entity will remember.`
`RECOMMENDATION: Break contact immediately. Trigger host personality resurgence.`

The moonlit garden had become a kill box. Ren felt the old War God instincts surge—the part of him that had fought these things across a hundred battlefields, the part that knew exactly how to kill a寄生 entity by severing its connection to the host's nervous system.
But that required power he didn't have. And killing the entity would kill Feng.

So he did the only thing he could: he reached out and pressed his thumb against the jade-banded ring on his own finger—the cheap thing he'd bought at a market stall the day before, laced with a subtle array pattern the Salvation System had helped him design.
The ring pulsed. A signal—too faint for the Patriarch's surveillance to catch, but strong enough to trigger the household's security motion sensors.
Garden lights blazed on.
Feng flinched, and the thing behind his eyes *recoiled*, sensitive to the sudden light. His body staggered, shook, and then his eyes rolled back and he dropped to his knees, gasping.
"Wha—" He looked up at Ren with tears streaming down his face. Feng—the real Feng, confused and terrified. "What happened? Why am I—why is there a knife—?"
Guards' footsteps pounded toward them from three directions.
Ren kicked the knife into the bushes and crouched beside Feng, one hand on his shoulder in the universal posture of concern.
"Easy. You sleepwalked. Came out here in a daze. I found you wandering."

It was the kind of lie that only worked if the person hearing it desperately wanted to believe it. Feng stared at him—for a moment with gratitude, then with suspicion, then with something that might have been the first fragile tendril of trust.
"Thank you," Feng whispered.
"Get some sleep," Ren said. "And Feng? If the dreams get worse... come to me. Not your father. Not your sister. *Me.*"
Feng nodded once and let the guards lead him away.
Ren stood alone in the garden, watching the lights go dark one by one. His hand was shaking—not from fear, but from rage. The thing in Feng's body had recognized him. It had said *I know you*.

In the previous timeline, the Abyss entities had learned his face in the final months of the war, when he'd become the last obstacle between them and a defenseless Earth. But that was three years from now. They shouldn't know him yet.
Unless the entity had access to something else—some conduit, some chink in reality that let it see further than it should.
The Salvation System offered no answers. Only a countdown:
`1090 days`
And a new alert:

`THREAD ADVANCED: T003 (Liang Feng's Spore Infection)`
`STATUS: Stage 2 confirmed. Entity contact made.`
`PROBLEM: The entity knows your face.`
`IMPLICATION: Your cover is not as secure as you believed.`

Ren walked back to his room on legs that wanted to run. Not from the estate. Not from the Liang family. From the memory of those gold-flecked eyes and the ancient, hungry voice that had slithered out of a young man's throat.
*I know you.*
The Abyss was closer than he'd thought. And it was learning.
He would have to be faster.
At 3 a.m., his phone buzzed. The unknown number.
*"The entity recognized you because you've fought before. In its time, you're already its enemy. The Abyss doesn't experience linear time the way we do."*
Ren typed back: *"You said you died in the future. Who were you?"*
A pause. Then:
*"I was your left flank at the Battle of the Eastern Gate. Liu Haoran. I held the line while you closed the first portal. You watched me die."*
Ren closed his eyes.

He remembered. A thin man with a scar across his jaw, laughing in the face of oblivion, holding a perimeter that shouldn't have held. He'd lasted eleven minutes against a tide that should have fallen in three. Those eleven minutes had given Ren the window to collapse the gate on three hundred Abyss creatures.
*"Liu Haoran is alive,"* Ren typed. *"He's twelve years old right now, living in the northern district."*

*"I know. I've been watching him. Making sure nothing changes the trajectory that turns him into a soldier."* A pause. *"I came back different, Zhou. I'm not entirely human anymore. But I'm still fighting the same war."*
*"Why contact me now?"*
*"Because the Patriarch is accelerating the timeline. He's found Node 5. And the entity inside Feng isn't the only one who knows your face."*
The line went dead.
Ren sat in the dark and revised his threat assessment. He wasn't the only one who'd come back. He wasn't the only one fighting the same war from a new angle.
And the enemy was moving faster than anyone had predicted.
One thousand ninety days.
